identity.title: Photographic Film, Paper, Plate, Chemical, and Copy Toner Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US manufacturers in the LMM band that repeatedly supply photographic or motion-picture film, sensitized paper or plates, photographic chemicals, copy toner, toner cartridges, or closely related contract coating and processing to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive production, shell entities, pure software or IP licensing, equipment-only businesses without repeat consumables or processing revenue, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat B2B sale of physical consumables and contract manufacturing or processing; some plate and consumables relationships are contract-based, while other volumes follow customers' print, imaging, and film usage.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation employment in the broader NAICS 325 chemical-manufacturing subsector, not 325992, while Eloundou et al. measure LLM task exposure across US occupations rather than this industry's wage-weighted task mix. I use the BLS mix to anchor the large manual/process share and apply lower exposure to production work than to administrative, sales, technical-documentation, planning, and quality-record work. The broad-industry bridge may overstate exposure if 325992 is more production-heavy, or understate it where imaging and formulation work is unusually digital.
a.rationale: Chemical manufacturing is 39.02% production occupations, including 12.28% chemical-equipment operators, 6.43% packaging/filling operators, and 3.33% inspectors/testers. Those physical and safety-sensitive tasks cap substitution, but planning, customer service, procurement, documentation, quality review, maintenance diagnostics, and machine-vision inspection create a meaningful exposed share. The general LLM study finds 15% of tasks can be accelerated by an LLM alone and 47%-56% with complementary software, so the estimate remains well below the software-enabled economy-wide upper range.
a.change_evidence: A 325992 establishment-level payroll by SOC, combined with time-and-motion task inventories for production, quality, maintenance, sales, planning, and administration and measured pre-existing automation, would materially move the range.
caveats: The occupation distribution is NAICS 325 rather than 325992 and is not wage-weighted here.
LLM exposure is not equivalent to labor substitution and does not identify tasks already automated.
The code spans film, plates, chemicals, and toner, whose plant automation and technical labor differ.

rho.rationale: Over five years, back-office copilots, demand and production planning, document generation, customer-support triage, predictive maintenance, machine vision, and anomaly detection should be implementable in portions of the exposed work. Implementation is constrained by small-firm capital budgets, legacy equipment and data, hazardous-process controls, quality validation, cybersecurity, product-change testing, and the need for accountable humans. DOE describes chemical AI controls as application-specific, data-complex, capital intensive, and sometimes low-return, supporting a moderate rather than high implementation share.
rho.change_evidence: Pilot results from five to ten independent 325992 plants showing labor hours before and after deployment, model exception rates, product-quality escapes, validation time, capex, and payback would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The DOE evidence concerns chemical and thermal processing broadly, not photographic materials or toner specifically.
The range assumes modern tools can connect to enough plant and ERP data without a full equipment replacement cycle.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is fundamentally physical consumables manufacturing, and Kodak's filing documents repeat plate, ink, chemical, film, toner, and processing relationships, including two-year plate contracts and consumables tied to installed equipment. Eligibility is reduced for equipment-only, captive, highly concentrated, nonrecurring development, licensing, and commodity production, as well as businesses whose normalized EBITDA does not represent a transferable operating firm.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 24 estimated LMM-band firms, classifying revenue by recurring consumables, repeat contract manufacturing or processing, one-time equipment, licensing, and customer concentration, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source measures eligibility among the injected LMM-firm population.
Some qualifying revenue may be product sales rather than conventionally labeled outsourced services.
The injected firm count is an estimate derived from size-class receipts and an industry margin bridge.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US business owners and employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers among LMM 325992 firms. I anchor the base to the 22% employer-business figure, with a wider range because manufacturing firms may be older and more transferable but plans may not close, transfers may be non-control, and strategic consolidation may add transactions absent from owner plans.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, rising to 30% among employer owners age 55 or older. More than half of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. These are intention measures, so the estimate allows for failed or nonqualifying transfers and for strategic transactions not anticipated by surveyed owners.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and sector brokers for US 325992 or named-product peers, matched to ownership age and control status over the last ten years, would materially move the range.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes firms far smaller than the LMM EBITDA band.
Surveyed intentions are not completed qualifying control transfers.
Internal family transfers and gifts may or may not meet the screen's control-transfer definition.

q.rationale: Consumables and recurring contract relationships allow an operator to retain part of an implemented gross benefit, especially where savings improve yield, uptime, quality, or service without changing customer specifications. Retention is limited by sophisticated OEM and commercial-print buyers, competitive bids, two-year contract renewals, declining legacy volumes, commodity input pass-through, and customer demands for lower unit prices. Kodak explicitly reports both recurring contracts and competitive pricing pressure.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level contracts and invoices showing indexation, cost-plus clauses, open-book pricing, renewal cadence, rebate terms, and realized price changes after documented productivity improvements would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Kodak is a large public-company proxy and may have bargaining power unlike LMM firms.
Retention varies sharply between proprietary film or formulations, contract coating, commodity toner, and plates.
The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation failure, which are represented elsewhere.

d5.bridge: Kodak is one diversified public producer rather than the full US 325992 LMM population, and its reported print challenges and product mix combine plates, toner systems, film, chemicals, inks, software, and services. I translate its disclosed print volume pressure, recurring consumables, concentrated film demand, and newer contract-coating applications into a constant-price, constant-quality quantity range. The proxy may understate niche film resilience or overstate demand where Kodak-specific brand and installed-base advantages matter.
d5.rationale: Legacy offset plates and toner face digital substitution and declining print volumes, while physical film, motion-picture use, specialty chemicals, printed electronics, battery-component coating, and repeat consumables provide offsets. Kodak reports print volume declines but also recurring plate and ink demand and growing industrial-film and chemical activity. The mixed basket supports modest contraction at base with a wide range around product mix.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of 325992 product-level shipment quantities and producer prices from Census and BLS, separated into film, plates, toner, cartridges, photographic chemicals, and contract coating, plus customer end-market forecasts, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Public data do not isolate constant-quality quantities for each product within 325992.
Kodak's portfolio includes activities outside 325992 and company-specific restructuring.
The range is sensitive to the weight of declining commercial print versus resilient film and emerging specialty applications.

o.rationale: Year-five demand for film, sensitized plates, toner, cartridges, and photographic chemicals still requires formulation, coating, converting, quality control, hazardous-material handling, and physical production by an accountable operator. Software and customers can automate ordering, workflow, and some image preparation, while process-free plates and digital workflows can eliminate some chemistry or steps, but they cannot supply the remaining physical basket without a manufacturer.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and product-roadmap evidence quantifying conversion to self-manufacture, all-digital substitutes, cartridge remanufacturing, process-free plates, and software-only workflows by major application would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The value is conditional on the demand remaining in d5; eliminated legacy demand is not counted twice.
A few large customers may vertically integrate or switch to substitute technologies faster than assumed.

narrative.executive_view: This is a heterogeneous physical-consumables industry: legacy print and toner volumes are pressured, but repeat film, chemical, plate, cartridge, and contract-coating work remains operator-intensive. AI is more likely to improve planning, documentation, quality inspection, maintenance, and customer workflows than to remove core coating and chemical production labor.
narrative.ai_impact: The largest near-term opportunities are scheduling and inventory decisions, specifications and batch-record review, sales and service support, predictive maintenance, machine-vision inspection, and anomaly detection. Chemical operations already have substantial automation, and application-specific data, capital, validation, and safety requirements slow conversion from technical exposure to implemented labor savings.
narrative.value_capture: Proprietary formulations, installed bases, repeat consumables, and contract production can preserve savings, but powerful buyers, competitive bids, input-cost mechanisms, and recurring contract renewals will share part of the benefit. Savings that improve yield, uptime, or quality are more retainable than visible reductions in direct labor per unit.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected population is only 24 estimated LMM-band firms. Many firms should have repeat external revenue, but equipment-only, captive, licensing-heavy, concentrated, or nonrecurring development businesses reduce eligibility. Cross-industry employer-owner evidence suggests a meaningful five-year transfer pool, but industry-specific completed-deal evidence is absent.
narrative.demand_durability: Commercial print plates and electrophotographic toner face continuing digital substitution and volume decline. Motion-picture and specialty film, photographic chemicals, repeat installed-base consumables, and emerging contract coating or specialty-material applications can offset part of that decline, leaving a mixed and product-dependent outlook.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal risks are accelerated print substitution, dependence on a few customers or installed platforms, raw-material volatility, environmental and product-quality obligations, legacy equipment, and a small eligible target universe. The evidence is dominated by broader-industry occupation data and a large public-company proxy rather than LMM 325992 observations.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat physical consumables and specialty coating or chemical know-how keep accountable operators necessary while AI improves supporting workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Legacy print and toner volume decline and a very small, heterogeneous firm universe can overwhelm incremental operating gains.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS 325992 industry definition
sources.supports: Defines 325992 as manufacturing sensitized film, paper, cloth and plates, toner and toner cartridges, and photographic chemicals.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 325 Chemical Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports production occupations at 39.02% of chemical-manufacturing employment, including chemical-equipment operators at 12.28%, packaging and filling operators at 6.43%, and inspectors/testers at 3.33%.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models
sources.supports: Finds about 15% of US worker tasks could be completed significantly faster with an LLM alone and 47%-56% with LLM-powered software, while explicitly not predicting adoption timing.

sources.title: DOE Advanced Manufacturing Office: Thermal Process Intensification Workshop Report
sources.supports: Documents AI and sensor opportunities in industrial process control and identifies chemical-manufacturing barriers including application-specific algorithms, complex data acquisition, capital intensity, testing, scale-up, disruption risk, and regulatory approval.

sources.title: Eastman Kodak Company 2024 Annual Report on Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes two-year recurring plate contracts, repeat ink and consumables, film and chemical lines, toner-based solutions, contract coating, print volume and pricing pressure, customer concentration, and physical production and quality obligations.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older; 22% of employer owners planned a sale or transfer within five years, rising to 30% among employer owners age 55 or older.
